turns.content: Hey, I'm stuck on a JSON parsing bottleneck in our service. What's the actual difference between using serde_json::RawValue and fully deserializing everything upfront?

Great question. The key distinction is that RawValue lets you hold onto the original parsed string structure without immediately converting it into concrete Rust types like String or HashMap. When you deserialize everything upfront, serde_json creates full instances of your structs right away, which takes time and memory. With RawValue, you can store the raw parts as-is until a specific field is actually accessed.

Wait, so I just keep everything as strings forever? That sounds like it would break my application logic eventually if I need to do arithmetic on those values later. Can't I still convert them when needed?

Exactly! You don't lock yourself into a single format. RawValue acts as an intermediate storage layer that can be lazily converted to String, f64, or other types on demand using the From trait. This means you only pay the conversion cost when someone actually reads that value, not every time the data structure is touched.

That still feels risky though. If I'm storing partial JSON fragments and then later decide to add a new field or change my schema, won't all those lazy conversions break? Isn't full deserialization safer for long-term maintainability?

You're right about safety in the short term, but consider this: your current service processes millions of requests per second. Storing fully deserialized structs for every request creates massive pressure on memory and CPU during runtime. RawValue allows you to defer that cost significantly. You only deserialize when a specific field is accessed by another part of your system.
But how do I actually implement this without writing custom code for every single field? Can't serde_json just handle the lazy conversion automatically if I tell it to?

Not entirely automatically in the way you're imagining. You need to manually wrap specific fields in RawValue when parsing, then implement a custom From trait for those fields so they can be converted later without triggering full deserialization again. However, once you set up that pattern, your main data structures stay lightweight until needed.

Oh wait... if I'm only converting when accessed, then my memory usage drops because I don't hold all the full structs in memory at once. And since my bottleneck was caused by too many allocations during parsing, this should fix it!

That's exactly the aha moment you were looking for! By using RawValue strategically and only deserializing on demand, you avoid premature allocation spikes. Your memory footprint shrinks because you're not materializing every possible field immediately, and your CPU usage drops since conversions happen lazily.

I'm trying to port an existing Python script that uses json.load with a custom parser to Rust. In Python I can just pass a regex pattern and it only extracts what I need, so I thought the same trick would work in Rust by writing my own deserializer for serde_json::Value instead of using full serialization.

That's a common misconception. In Rust you can't just write a custom deserializer to skip fields without paying the cost of parsing them first unless you actually use lazy evaluation patterns like serde_json::RawValue or implement your own parser that builds a tree incrementally.
Oh, so I can't just avoid deserializing everything? That sounds expensive. Is there a way to deserialize only specific fields without loading the whole JSON into memory?

You need to use lazy deserialization patterns like serde_json::RawValue or implement your own parser that builds a tree incrementally, and then you can access individual fields on demand instead of loading everything upfront.
Wait, so I should be using something like RawValue? But isn't that just the raw JSON string?

RawValue is different from serde_json::RawValue; it's a special type in Rust that holds a serialized value without full deserialization, allowing you to access fields on demand. You can use this with traits like Deserialize and Serialize to implement lazy loading.

I'm trying to move a large JSON payload into our Rust service, but we're hitting memory limits. I know serde_json::Value is fine for small data, but what's the best way to handle huge files without loading everything at once?

The standard approach is to use lazy deserialization with serde_json::RawValue so you only parse the fields your code actually needs. You can wrap individual values in a RawValue struct and then call deserialize on demand when the application logic requests them, which avoids allocating the full tree upfront.
Wait, I thought that was just for small metadata? I'm worried about the overhead of repeatedly calling deserialize on RawValue. Is it really faster than loading the whole document?
It is significantly faster because you skip parsing large arrays and nested objects entirely until a specific field is accessed, which drastically reduces memory usage and CPU time for big payloads.

I'm trying to parse a huge JSON blob into a struct but I keep hitting memory limits because serde_json deserializes everything at once. My code looks like this: let data = serde_json::from_str(json_string); and it crashes on large files. Is there a way to only deserialize specific fields without loading the whole payload?

You're running into the classic problem of eager deserialization where serde_json::Deserializer parses every token immediately. The solution is lazy deserialization using serde_json::RawValue which holds strings as-is until you explicitly convert them. Instead of calling from_str on the whole string, parse just the fields you need and keep the rest as RawValue so they don't expand in memory.
Okay, but how do I actually get a specific field without parsing everything? Like if my struct has a nested object with many optional fields, can I grab just the id and leave the rest as lazy?

Yes, you use serde_json::RawValue to represent unparsed JSON fragments. You can manually parse only the keys you need using a minimal deserializer or regex, then store the remaining parts in RawValue fields of your struct. This way those nested objects stay as strings until someone explicitly calls deserialize on them later.
That sounds complicated though. Can I just write a custom deserializer trait that implements something like serde::Deserialize but only for specific fields? Or do I need to manually parse the JSON?

You don't need a whole new Deserialize trait since RawValue already implements Deserialize<serde_json::de::Value> when you choose to convert it. But for true laziness, you keep the value as RawValue in your struct and only trigger deserialization when that field is actually accessed in code. This avoids the full parse cost until necessary.
So my struct would hold some regular fields and others as serde_json::RawValue, right? And then when I do let result = deserialize(&data) it only parses what I told it to?

Not quite. RawValue doesn't parse automatically; you must explicitly call .as_str() or .into_string() on a RawValue when you want the content as a string, or use serde_json::from_value if you convert it to a Value first. The laziness comes from not converting until runtime access.
Ah! So I parse only the top-level fields I need and store everything else as RawValue, then only convert those when my application actually needs them? That means no big upfront parse?

Exactly. You avoid loading the entire JSON into memory by parsing selectively and using serde_json::RawValue for the rest of the structure, so your process stays lightweight until those lazy fields are accessed.

I'm struggling with a book exercise where I need to parse JSON without loading the entire file into memory, and I don't want to use serde_json::from_str for everything. Is there a way to deserialize only specific fields lazily?

That's a great instinct for performance in Rust. The library provides a RawValue type that lets you treat raw JSON strings as opaque values until needed. You can store these RawValue instances in your structure and only convert them to actual types when the consumer requests them, similar to how you might cache data instead of recomputing it every time. Have you considered using Option<serde_json::RawValue> for fields where deserialization isn't immediate?
I've looked at serde_json::RawValue but I'm not sure how to handle the trait bounds and conversion logic properly when mixing RawValue with other types in my struct.

Think of it like handling a sealed envelope: you keep the sealed package (the RawValue) until someone asks to open it, then you extract the letter. When implementing your struct's From trait or using serde attributes, you only need to derive Deserialize for the fields that are actually deserialized immediately, while leaving others as RawValue placeholders.
Okay, so I should use derive macro on some fields but not all? And what about serialization when sending this data back out?

Yes, you apply derive to only those fields that need immediate conversion during deserialization. For the outgoing direction, serde_json will serialize RawValue as a string or object node automatically if configured correctly. Do you have a specific scenario where you're receiving JSON from an external API and want to avoid full parsing?
